json_data.html:
Zuar · Field Guide
Whitepaper · 2026 Edition

The AI-Native Analytics Portal

Embedded, headless, and agent-ready. A technical field guide to the modern Data Experience Platform — where governed pipelines, white-label delivery, and AI agents finally meet in one architecture.

PF Patrick Fox
Independent technical perspective
~12 min read Reference architecture: Zuar
Force 01
Embedded & White-Label
Analytics ships inside your product and your brand — not as a separate tool people have to go visit.
Force 02
Headless & Composable
Governed data, defined once and exposed through APIs, consumed by any surface — human or machine.
Force 03
Agentic & MCP-Ready
AI agents that query, build, and operate analytics through an open, governed protocol.
01Executive Summary 02The Shift 03Reference Architecture 04The Pipeline Layer 05The Experience Layer 06Headless & Composable 07AI & MCP 08Security & Governance 09Who It's For 10Build vs. Buy
Executive Summary

01 —Dashboards are becoming data experiences

For two decades, business intelligence meant a tool you logged into, a seat you paid for, and a dashboard most people never opened. That model is breaking down on three fronts at once.

The first shift is delivery. Insight increasingly has to live where the work happens — inside a SaaS product, a customer portal, a partner extranet — wearing the host brand, not a vendor's. The second is architecture: organizations are pulling metric definitions out of monolithic BI tools and into a governed, API-first "headless" layer that any surface can consume. The third, and newest, is autonomy: AI agents that don't just answer questions but assemble queries, build pages, and operate the analytics stack on a person's behalf.

These forces converge on a single architectural idea — the Data Experience Platform (DXP): a stack that unifies the pipeline that moves and models data, the experience layer that delivers it under your brand, and an open interface that lets both humans and machines work with it safely. Zuar — an Austin-based analytics company founded in 2015 — is one of the clearest expressions of this pattern in market today, pairing Zuar Runner (data pipelines) with Zuar Portal (embedded, customer-facing analytics), and publicly positioning the whole platform around AI, MCP, and agentic workflows.

This guide is an independent walk through that architecture: what each layer does, why headless and embedded reinforce each other, and why the Model Context Protocol — an open standard introduced by Anthropic in late 2024 — is the connective tissue that turns a governed analytics platform into something an AI agent can actually drive.

The thesis in one line

The winning analytics platform is no longer the one with the prettiest dashboard. It is the one that can be embedded anywhere, composed by anything, and operated by an agent — without giving up governance.

  • Embed, don't redirect — analytics inside your product, in your brand.
  • Define once, consume everywhere — governed metrics over APIs.
  • Open the platform to agents — safely, through MCP.
  • Govern the whole loop — permissions, validation, version control.
Market Context

02 —Three forces reshaping BI

None of these is hypothetical. Each is already a named product category with serious vendors behind it — together they describe where analytics is going.

Embedded analytics

SaaS and data-product teams are putting dashboards inside their own applications under their own brand. The portal — multi-tenant, white-labeled, SSO-secured — has become the default delivery surface for customer-facing data.

Delivery

Headless & semantic layers

Cube, the dbt Semantic Layer, GoodData and others expose governed metric definitions over REST, GraphQL and SQL — "define once, consume everywhere." The presentation layer becomes a choice, not a cage.

Architecture

Agentic analytics

ThoughtSpot's Spotter, Tableau's "Agentic Analytics," and Google Looker's dashboard agents all point one way: from natural-language questions toward agents that build and act. Authoring, not just answering.

Autonomy

The center of gravity is moving from "which dashboard do I open?" to "what do I want, and which surface — or agent — delivers it?"

Reference Architecture

03 —One pipeline, two kinds of consumer

The modern stack is a straight line with a fork at the end. Data is moved and modeled once, governed in the middle, and then consumed by people and by machines — through the same secured surface.

The Pipeline Layer

04 —Zuar Runner — get the data right, automatically

Runner (formerly Mitto) is Zuar's automated ELT engine: it pulls data from your sources, stages it in a warehouse, and shapes it for analytics — on a schedule or on demand. Everything downstream depends on this layer being dependable.

Connectors, not custom code

Pre-built connectors for the systems analytics actually runs on — Salesforce, NetSuite, Google Analytics, HubSpot, MongoDB, S3, Tableau and more — with 25+ pre-built connectors surfaced on the product page.

Ingest

Transform in place

Pivot, aggregate, pre-calculate and create tables with SQL — and because Runner is dbt-compatible, teams already invested in dbt can bring those models along instead of rewriting them.

Model

Stage to any warehouse

Land data in Snowflake, BigQuery, AWS, Azure, MySQL, Exasol — or Runner's built-in Postgres. Original source data is retained for troubleshooting and reprocessing, not discarded after load.

Stage

Schedule or trigger

Run jobs on a schedule or fire them from an external webhook — event-driven pipelines that react the moment upstream data lands, rather than waiting for the next nightly window.

Orchestrate

Cloud, Docker, or your code

Run hosted by Zuar or on-premise via Docker. Drop in custom Python in isolated virtual environments when a source needs logic a connector can't express.

Deploy

Enterprise auth, by default

Support for the major API-based authentication methods — SAML, OIDC, OAuth, IdP — with SSL encryption in transit, so pipelines meet the access controls security teams expect.

Secure
Vendor-reported

Zuar reports platform uptime of 99.99% and says dynamic schema adjustment can cut job run times by up to 40%. As with any vendor figures, treat these as Zuar's own claims rather than independent benchmarks — but they signal where the product is tuned to compete: reliability and throughput.

The Experience Layer

05 —Zuar Portal — analytics in your brand, for your customers

Portal is a low-code builder for customer-facing and embedded analytics: a branded web application that consolidates dashboards and data apps from across your BI stack into a single, governed surface.

Pages, grids & blocks

Assemble data-rich applications by drag-and-drop. The atomic unit is the block — a full HTML/CSS/JavaScript surface with access to query results, theme variables, user context and the auth layer.

Build

Embed Tableau, Power BI & more

Bring Tableau (Cloud or Server), Microsoft Power BI, ThoughtSpot and custom web visualizations into one branded experience — including fully white-labeled Tableau.

Consolidate

White-label, end to end

Your logo, your colors, your domain. Themes restyle every surface from a central palette, so embedded analytics looks like a native part of your product — never a bolted-on vendor tool.

Brand

SSO & role-based access

Secure single sign-on with role-based personalization: each user — and each tenant — sees only the analytics meant for them. That row- and role-scoped access is what makes true multi-tenant customer portals possible.

Govern

JavaScript API & filters

A documented JS API drives interactivity and dynamic filtering across data sources, so a control in one block can reshape every chart on the page — the foundation for genuinely interactive data apps, not static reports.

Interact

Asset Manager

A built-in repository for images, PDFs, logos and documents — so the content around your analytics (onboarding guides, datasheets, brand assets) lives in the same governed place as the dashboards.

Manage
Architecture Deep-Dive

06 —Headless & composable — why it matters now

"Headless" means the analytics platform has no single mandatory front-end. Governed definitions live in a semantic layer and are exposed through APIs; the presentation — a dashboard, your app, a pipeline, or an agent — becomes a consumer, not the system itself.

Define once, consume everywhere

A semantic layer is the governed model of your business: what "revenue," "active customer," or "on-time" actually mean, in one place. Headless BI exposes that model over REST, GraphQL and SQL so every consumer computes the same numbers. The metric stops being re-implemented — slightly differently — in every tool.

From a destination to a service

This is the quiet but decisive change: analytics stops being a place you go and becomes a service other things call. That is exactly what makes embedding clean (your app queries governed metrics with tenant-scoped access), makes pipelines composable, and — critically — makes the platform legible to machines.

Where Zuar fits

Portal's block model is a composability primitive: a sandboxed surface with first-class access to governed query results, theme, and user context. Pair that with Runner's modeled warehouse and a JavaScript API, and the portal behaves less like a dashboard tool and more like a programmable analytics application platform.

The Headline

07 —AI & MCP — making the platform agent-ready

An agent is only as useful as what it can safely reach. The Model Context Protocol is how a governed analytics platform becomes something an AI agent can actually operate — not by scraping a UI, but by calling real, permissioned capabilities.

What MCP is

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard, open-sourced by Anthropic in November 2024, for connecting AI applications to external tools and data through one universal interface. Before MCP, every AI-to-system connection was a bespoke integration — an N×M tangle of custom glue. MCP replaces that with a single protocol of hosts (the AI app), clients, and servers (which expose tools and data). It has since been adopted across the industry, including by OpenAI and Google in 2025.

Why analytics needs it

A headless platform already exposes governed capabilities over APIs. MCP gives an agent a standard, discoverable way to use them: list the datasources, run a governed query, author a block, assemble a page — each as a typed tool call with real arguments, inside the platform's existing permission model. The agent isn't guessing at a screen; it's operating the system through its own front door.

Zuar's public direction

Zuar now brands itself around "Data Experience & AI Analytics," and publicly describes MCP and RAG as backend components that connect AI models to governed business data, alongside a builder agent that can generate working dashboards and AI-assisted authoring with code-assist LLMs — security "built in, not bolted on." The block model is precisely what makes that feasible: a sandboxed surface an agent can compose within guardrails.

The platforms that win the agent era won't be the ones that bolt a chatbot onto a dashboard. They'll be the ones an agent can safely build inside.

Security & Governance

08 —The part that makes all of it shippable

Embedding multiplies your blast radius. Agents multiply your action space. Both make governance the precondition for everything else — not an afterthought.

Identity & access

SSO over standard protocols (SAML, OAuth/OIDC, LDAP) with role-based access and tenant isolation, so the right person — and the right customer — sees exactly the right slice, and nothing more.

Data in transit & at rest

Encryption in transit by default and warehouse-grade protection at rest. Runner retains source data for reprocessing, which makes recovery and lineage tractable rather than a forensic exercise.

Governed AI, not loose AI

The decisive idea in Zuar's framing is that AI is governed by default: AI-generated SQL, JavaScript and Python run inside the portal's existing security framework — so agents inherit the same permission model as people, never a privileged side door.

Validation & version control

Changes — whether authored by a person or an agent — can be validated for structure and dangling references and tracked in version control, so the analytics layer is reviewable, diff-able and reversible like any other production code.

Where It Lands

09 —Who this architecture is for

The same stack solves four very different problems — which is exactly the point of a composable platform.

SaaS & ISVs embedding analytics

Ship a polished, multi-tenant analytics experience inside your product without building — and forever maintaining — a bespoke charting app. White-label it so it reads as a native feature, and let each customer see only their own data.

Product teams

Enterprises building data products

Stand up governed, self-service portals for internal teams and external partners on top of a modeled warehouse — one definition of the metrics, many audiences, consistent numbers everywhere.

Data & platform teams

Analytics teams adopting AI

Give data-savvy analysts AI-assisted authoring — SQL, visualization JavaScript, Python transforms — that runs inside the governance layer, turning a small team into full-stack BI application builders.

Analysts & engineers

Consultancies & agencies

Deliver branded analytics portals to clients fast, on a repeatable platform instead of a snowflake codebase per engagement — with the pipeline, delivery and governance already solved.

Service providers
Decision Framework

10 —Build it yourself, or stand on a platform?

A custom analytics portal is buildable — the question is whether differentiated value lives in rebuilding auth, theming, embedding and pipelines, or in the data product on top. A qualitative comparison, free of invented numbers:

Dimension Build your own Platform (Zuar)
Time to first portal Quarters — auth, theming, embedding and pipelines are all from scratch Weeks — assemble on primitives that already exist
Data pipeline Build and operate ELT, scheduling and connectors yourself Runner: connectors, transforms, scheduling, webhooks included
Embedding BI Hand-roll Tableau / Power BI / ThoughtSpot integration and SSO Native embedding plus white-label, including Tableau
Multi-tenant security Design and certify role- and row-level isolation in-house Role-based personalization and SSO as platform features
AI / agent readiness A research project on top of an already-large project Publicly signaled AI direction: MCP / RAG / builder agent
Ongoing maintenance A standing engineering team owns the whole surface forever Maintained by the vendor; your team owns the data product
Where your effort goes Rebuilding undifferentiated infrastructure The analytics and experience that are actually yours

Directional comparison for decision-making, not a benchmark. The right answer depends on your team, timeline and how much of the stack is genuinely core to your product.

Analytics that you embed, compose, and let agents operate

That is the shape of the modern data stack — and it is buildable today. Explore Zuar's platform, or take the architecture in this guide and pressure-test it against your own.
